1: A shadow: YHWH speaks to Moses again regarding Aaron and his sons. The divine address that opens each major priestly instruction anticipates the one High Priest who needs no such instruction — Christ speaks and acts from his own divine authority (Matthew 7:29: 'he taught them as one who had authority, and not as their scribes').
2: A direct type: 'Tell Aaron and his sons to keep away from the holy things of the people of Israel, which they dedicate to me, so that they do not profane my holy name.' The priests must guard the holy name from profanation. Christ is the one who hallows the Father's name — his entire life was the anti-profanation, the perfect glorification of the holy name: 'I glorified you on earth, having accomplished the work that you gave me to do' (John 17:4). The holy name that Israel's priests must not profane is the name that is above every name, given to Christ at his exaltation (Philippians 2:9-11).
3: A direct type: 'Say to them: Throughout your generations, if any one of all your offspring approaches the holy things that the people of Israel dedicate to YHWH, while he has an uncleanness, that person shall be cut off from my presence: I am YHWH.' The threat of being cut off (nikrat) for approaching holiness in uncleanness is the negative framing of the entire sacrificial system's problem. But Christ opens the way: 'We have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus, by the new and living way that he opened for us through the curtain, that is, through his flesh' (Hebrews 10:19-20).
4: A shadow: The priest with skin disease (tzaraat) or bodily discharge must not eat from holy offerings until cleansed. The conditions that disqualify a priest parallel those that Christ healed in his ministry — he touched the leper (Matthew 8:3), healed the woman with a chronic discharge (Luke 8:43-48). The diseases that excluded from priestly service, Christ reversed.
5: A shadow: Contact with creeping things or unclean persons spreads impurity. The graduated uncleanness system establishes that holiness is vulnerable to contamination — the opposite is true for Christ: 'the power of the Lord was with him to heal' (Luke 5:17); when Christ touched the unclean, they became clean rather than him becoming unclean (Mark 1:41-42).
6: A direct type: 'Such a person shall be unclean until the evening and shall not eat of the holy things unless he has bathed his body in water.' The bathing in water and waiting until evening structure a death-to-restoration cycle: evening = the death-and-burial moment, sunrise = restoration. Baptism is the Christian form of this: 'We were buried therefore with him by baptism into death, in order that, just as Christ was raised from the dead by the glory of the Father, we too might walk in newness of life' (Romans 6:4).
7: A shadow: 'When the sun goes down he shall be clean, and afterward he may eat of the holy things, for they are his food.' Restoration after cleansing comes with the ability to eat the holy food. The forgiven sinner restored to fellowship with God feeds on Christ: 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35); the parable of the prodigal son: 'let us eat and celebrate' (Luke 15:23).
8: A direct type: 'He shall not eat what dies of itself or is torn by beasts, and so make himself unclean by it: I am YHWH.' An animal that dies of natural causes or is killed by a predator is excluded — only intentional sacrifice is acceptable. Christ's death was neither natural nor random: 'I lay down my life of my own accord. No one takes it from me, but I lay it down of my own accord. I have authority to lay it down, and I have authority to take it up again' (John 10:17-18). The prohibition on animals that die passively is the negative image of Christ's active self-offering.
9: A shadow: 'They shall therefore keep my charge, lest they bear sin for it and die thereby when they profane it: I am YHWH who sanctifies them.' The priestly warning — bear sin and die — establishes that priestly negligence has fatal consequences. Christ bore the priests' own failures along with the people's: 'he is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world' (1 John 2:2).
10: A direct type: 'A lay person shall not eat of a holy thing; no foreign guest of the priest, nor a hired worker, shall eat of a holy thing.' The outsider (zar) excluded from sacred food is the type of the Gentile under the old covenant. Paul traces the abolition of this exclusion to Christ: 'He has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility... that he might create in himself one new man in place of the two, so making peace' (Ephesians 2:14-15).
11: A direct type: 'But if a priest buys a slave as his property for money, the slave may eat of it, and anyone born in his house may eat of his food.' The purchased slave, bought into the priestly household, gains access to holy food. 'You were bought with a price' (1 Corinthians 6:20; Leviticus 7:23); those purchased by Christ's blood become members of his household and share his food. The Eucharist is the holy food of the priestly household to which the bought-and-redeemed now belong.
12: A shadow: 'If a priest's daughter marries a layman, she shall not eat of the contribution of the holy things.' Alliance with the outsider costs access to the sacred table. 'You cannot drink the cup of the Lord and the cup of demons. You cannot partake of the table of the Lord and the table of demons' (1 Corinthians 10:21). Divided allegiance forfeits the sacred table.
13: A direct type: 'But if a priest's daughter is widowed or divorced and has no child and returns to her father's house, as in her youth she may eat of her father's food.' Return to the father's house restores access — a miniature of the parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15:17-22). The father runs to meet the returning son and restores him fully: 'bring the best robe and put it on him.' Those who return to the Father through Christ regain full access to the Father's table.
14: A shadow: 'If anyone eats of a holy thing unintentionally, he shall add a fifth to it and give the holy thing to the priest.' The chamesh (one-fifth) addition for unintentional violations of sacred food mirrors the guilt offering's restitution principle. The inadvertent profaning of holy things still requires restitution — reinforcing that holiness cannot simply be ignored, even accidentally.
15: A shadow: 'The priests shall not profane the holy things of the people of Israel, which they contribute to YHWH.' The priest's responsibility to guard the holy things is the priestly vocation's core: preservation and presentation of holiness. Christ as high priest guards and presents his holy church: 'I protected them in your name, which you have given me. I have guarded them, and not one of them has been lost' (John 17:12).
16: A shadow: 'And so cause them to bear iniquity and guilt, by eating their holy things: for I am YHWH who sanctifies them.' Priestly negligence that allows the unqualified to eat holy food loads them with guilt. Christ as the faithful high priest never loads his people with guilt but removes it: 'Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest' (Matthew 11:28).
17: A direct type: YHWH says the burnt offering is accepted from Israel or from any resident alien (ger). The inclusion of the resident alien in acceptable offerings is a type of Gentile inclusion in the new covenant. Christ explicitly extends the gospel to all: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations' (Matthew 28:19). The alien welcomed at the altar becomes the Gentile welcomed into the church.
18: A direct type: 'For you to be accepted it must be a male without blemish [tamim], of the bulls or the sheep or the goats.' The tamim requirement extended to include resident aliens — the perfection standard is universal. Whether offered by Israelite or alien, the same unblemished male is required. 'There is neither Jew nor Greek... for you are all one in Christ Jesus' (Galatians 3:28) — and Christ, the unblemished substitute, represents all equally.
19: A shadow: The requirement of a perfect animal for any free-will or vow offering reinforces that no human religious effort — however willing or devoted — can substitute for a perfect sacrifice. The freewill offering's tamim requirement means even enthusiastic devotion cannot lower the perfection standard.
20: A direct type: 'You shall not offer anything that has a blemish, for it will not be acceptable for you.' The categorical rejection of the blemished offering — it will not (lo yeratze) be accepted — establishes that God does not lower his standards. Christ meets the standard that no animal can sustain: 'a lamb without blemish or spot, foreknown before the foundation of the world' (1 Peter 1:19-20).
21: A direct type: 'And when anyone offers a sacrifice of peace offerings to YHWH to fulfill a vow or as a freewill offering from the herd or from the flock, to be accepted it must be perfect [tamim]; there shall be no blemish in it.' The peace offering's tamim requirement points to Christ as the one who makes perfect peace: 'he himself is our peace' (Ephesians 2:14); 'making peace by the blood of his cross' (Colossians 1:20). The unblemished peace offering is the type of the one who could make genuine peace — not temporary truce but the removal of enmity itself.
22: A direct type: 'Animals blind or disabled or mutilated or having a discharge or an itch or scabs you shall not offer to YHWH or give them to YHWH as a food offering on the altar.' The catalog of disqualifying blemishes is the negative image of Christ's perfection. Each defect listed is one Christ did not have: no spiritual blindness (John 9:39-41: he gave sight to the blind), no brokenness of character (Hebrews 4:15), no moral discharge. Isaiah 53:9: 'He had done no violence, and there was no deceit in his mouth.'
23: A shadow: 'An ox or a sheep that has a part too long or too short you may present for a freewill offering, but for a vow offering it will not be accepted.' The graduated acceptance — freewill but not vow — shows that the most binding offerings require the highest perfection. Vows (binding commitments) require tamim; the new covenant is the most binding of all vows, and it required the most perfect of all sacrifices (Hebrews 9:15).
24: A shadow: 'Any animal that has its testicles bruised or crushed or torn or cut you shall not offer to YHWH; you shall not do it within your land.' The prohibition on sexually mutilated animals reflects the covenant's concern for wholeness and integrity in what is offered. What is offered to God must be whole — not damaged, not compromised. Christ offered himself whole, without diminishment: 'holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners' (Hebrews 7:26).
25: A shadow: 'Neither shall you offer as the bread of your God any such animals gotten from a foreigner. Since there is a blemish in them, because of their mutilation, they will not be accepted for you.' The consistent standard applied to foreign-sourced animals prevents any double standard. God does not accept a lower quality from Gentile offerings. The standards are universal — and Christ, from the nation of Israel, met the universal standard on behalf of all nations.
26: A direct type: 'And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying, When an ox or sheep or goat is born, it shall remain seven days with its mother, and from the eighth day on it shall be acceptable as a food offering to YHWH.' The eight-day principle: seven days = completion of creation week; the eighth day = the beginning of new creation. Circumcision was also on the eighth day (Leviticus 12:3) — entrance into the covenant on the first day of the new week. Christ rose on the first day of the new week — the eighth day — inaugurating the new creation: 'If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation' (2 Corinthians 5:17).
27: A shadow: 'But you shall not kill an ox or a sheep and her young in one day.' The prohibition against slaughtering a mother and her young together on the same day reflects YHWH's mercy even toward animals (cf. Deuteronomy 22:6-7). The merciful character of the God who commands these sacrifices points to the mercy behind the cross: 'God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8).
28: A shadow: The instruction regarding the slaughter of the animal and its young on the same day. YHWH's mercy toward animals (Proverbs 12:10: 'A righteous man cares for the needs of his animal') is a dim reflection of his mercy toward humanity, which he demonstrates fully in Christ.
29: A direct type: 'And when you sacrifice a sacrifice of thanksgiving to YHWH, you shall sacrifice it so that you may be accepted.' The thanksgiving offering (todah) is the paradigmatic response-offering — not for sin or obligation but for gratitude. Christ himself offered the ultimate todah: 'Father, I thank you that you have heard me' (John 11:41); at the Last Supper, 'he took the cup and gave thanks' (eucharistēsas, Luke 22:17) — the Eucharist is the new todah.
30: A direct type: 'It shall be eaten on the same day; you shall leave none of it until morning: I am YHWH.' The thanksgiving offering must be consumed the same day — no delay, no storage. The urgency of responding to God's saving act: 'Behold, now is the favorable time; behold, now is the day of salvation' (2 Corinthians 6:2). The gospel is not a gift to be deferred — it is to be received immediately.
31: A shadow: 'So you shall keep my commandments and do them: I am YHWH.' The repeated obedience call. The obedience of the entire chapter — purity of priest, perfection of offering, urgency of response — finds its fulfillment in Christ who 'learned obedience through what he suffered' (Hebrews 5:8) and thereby became 'the source of eternal salvation to all who obey him' (Hebrews 5:9).
32: A direct type: 'And you shall not profane my holy name, that I may be sanctified among the people of Israel. I am YHWH who sanctifies you [mekadishchem].' The sanctification formula closes the chapter: YHWH is the one who sanctifies (mekadesh). This is the OT ground of the NT's repeated claim that Christ sanctifies: 'we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all' (Hebrews 10:10); 'he who sanctifies and those who are sanctified all have one source' (Hebrews 2:11). The God who said 'I am YHWH who sanctifies you' accomplished that sanctification through his Son's body.
33: A direct type: '...who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God: I am YHWH.' The chapter closes with the exodus-redemption formula. The Levitical law rests on the Exodus: Israel's obedience is the response to prior redemption, not the condition of it. Christ accomplished the greater Exodus: Moses and Elijah at the Transfiguration 'spoke of his departure [exodon], which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem' (Luke 9:31). The one who brought Israel out of Egypt is the one who brings humanity out of the slavery of sin.
